q: What does CI/CD automate, and why run it on every change?
a: It automates the journey from commit to production (build, test, deploy) and runs on every change so problems surface immediately while changes are small — making releases routine, frequent, and low-risk instead of rare and scary.

q: Distinguish Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment.
a: CI: auto build + test every change (catch integration/regressions early). Continuous Delivery: CI plus always keeping changes in a deployable state, with a human approving the prod release. Continuous Deployment: every passing change auto-releases to prod, no manual approval.

q: What is the single step that differs between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment?
a: The final production release: Delivery keeps a human in the loop (push-button approval); Deployment removes it (fully automatic to prod). Both need strong automated tests.

q: Why is CI a practice, not just a build server?
a: It requires developers to actually integrate small changes into the shared mainline frequently (trunk-based development), behind a green build. A build server with long-lived branches isn't 'continuous' integration.

q: What is the test pyramid and how should it shape pipeline stages?
a: Many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests, very few slow end-to-end tests at the top. Order pipeline stages cheap/fast first (lint → build → unit → integration → e2e/scan) so failures surface in seconds — fail fast.

q: Why are slow and flaky CI pipelines dangerous beyond annoyance?
a: Slow pipelines make people batch changes and stop integrating continuously; flaky tests train people to 'just re-run it', so they ignore real failures too. The result is an ignored pipeline that gives false confidence.

q: What does 'build once, promote the same artifact' mean and why?
a: CI builds an immutable, versioned artifact (e.g. an image tagged with the Git SHA) once, then carries that exact artifact through every stage/environment. Rebuilding per environment means you didn't test what you shipped.

q: Name techniques to make CI fast.
a: Caching dependencies/Docker layers between runs; running independent jobs in parallel (and sharding test suites); path filters/affected-target detection to run only what changed; matrix builds for parallel version/OS combos.

q: In GitHub Actions, what is the workflow → job → step hierarchy?
a: A workflow (YAML in .github/workflows) is triggered by an event (push, pull_request, schedule, manual) and contains jobs; jobs run in parallel on fresh runners (order them with needs:); each job is a sequence of steps (run: commands or uses: Actions).

q: Jobs run on fresh runners — how do you pass a build output to a later job?
a: Upload it as an artifact (actions/upload-artifact) in one job and download it (actions/download-artifact) in a later job linked via needs:. Caches (actions/cache) speed up inputs like dependencies across runs.

q: Secrets vs variables in GitHub Actions — when to use each?
a: Secrets are encrypted and masked in logs — for credentials/tokens (${{ secrets.X }}). Variables are non-sensitive, visible config (${{ vars.X }}). Prefer OIDC over storing long-lived cloud secrets at all.

q: What does promoting through environments (dev → staging → prod) look like done right?
a: The same immutable artifact flows through each environment, with only config changing per environment. Production sits behind an approval gate (Continuous Delivery). You ship exactly what you tested.

q: Compare rolling, blue-green, and canary deployments.
a: Rolling: replace instances gradually, no extra infra (k8s default). Blue-green: two full environments, test green then switch traffic at once — instant rollback, needs double capacity. Canary: send a small % of traffic to the new version, watch metrics, then ramp or roll back.

q: When would you choose blue-green vs canary?
a: Blue-green when you need instant cutover/rollback and can afford double capacity. Canary for high-traffic, high-stakes services where you want to validate against a small slice of real traffic (with good metrics) before full rollout.

q: Why is rollback a first-class concern, and how is it done in Kubernetes?
a: Things break in prod that passed every test, so rollback must be fast and practiced. In k8s, `kubectl rollout undo` scales the previous ReplicaSet back up — instant. Pair with post-deploy verification (golden signals) to detect bad deploys quickly.

q: What is GitOps and how does rollback work in it?
a: Git is the single source of truth for desired state; an in-cluster controller (Argo CD/Flux) continuously reconciles the cluster to match. CI commits the new image tag to Git; the controller applies it. Rollback is a `git revert` — the controller reconciles back.

q: Why is the CI/CD pipeline an especially high-value attack target?
a: It's privileged, trusted automation with access to source, secrets, registries, and production. Compromising it (or a dependency/Action it runs) is a supply-chain attack — owning the pipeline means owning everything it ships.

q: What does OIDC give you over a stored long-lived cloud secret?
a: The pipeline exchanges a short-lived, workload-identity token with the cloud at runtime (tied to this repo/workflow), so there are no long-lived cloud keys to leak or rotate — the biggest CI/CD security upgrade for cloud deploys.

q: Why pin third-party Actions/images to a commit SHA instead of a version tag?
a: Tags are mutable — a hijacked or compromised action republished under the same tag would run inside your trusted pipeline and could exfiltrate secrets. Pinning to a full commit SHA freezes the exact code you reviewed.

q: Give three CI/CD supply-chain hardening practices.
a: Pin Actions/images to SHAs from trusted sources; scan & pin dependencies (fail on critical CVEs); restrict untrusted-PR/fork workflows from secrets; sign artifacts (cosign) + generate an SBOM; isolate/ephemeral self-hosted runners.

q: How should workflow permissions and production deploys be locked down?
a: Default the workflow token to read-only and grant only what each job needs; put production behind an Environment with required reviewers and environment-scoped secrets; enforce branch protection (PR review + green CI, no force-push to main).

q: What are pipeline 'gates' and why encode them as required checks?
a: Automated quality/security conditions a change must pass (tests, coverage, lint, no critical vulns, no secrets, policy-as-code like tfsec/OPA). Making them required status checks means quality and security are enforced automatically, not left to trust.

q: Composite action vs reusable workflow — what's the difference?
a: A composite action bundles several STEPS into one action called with `uses:`. A reusable workflow is a whole workflow (jobs) called via `workflow_call`. Steps vs jobs/pipelines.

q: Why pin third-party GitHub Actions to a commit SHA instead of a tag?
a: A moving tag like @v4 can be re-pointed at malicious code by a compromised maintainer; a full commit SHA is immutable. It's a supply-chain control.

q: Why are ephemeral self-hosted runners safer than persistent ones for PR builds?
a: A persistent runner can be poisoned by one job (leftover files, cached creds) affecting the next. Ephemeral runners give each job a fresh, single-use machine that's destroyed after — no cross-job contamination.

q: What is an SBOM and what does it let you answer?
a: A Software Bill of Materials lists every dependency in an artifact (CycloneDX/SPDX). It lets you answer 'are we affected by CVE-X?' in seconds instead of auditing manually.

q: What do cosign and SLSA add to a pipeline?
a: cosign (Sigstore) signs artifacts/images and attaches signed attestations (SBOM, provenance), verified at deploy. SLSA is a framework grading how tamper-resistant the build is. Together the pipeline cryptographically vouches for what it ships.

q: How does progressive delivery differ from a manually-promoted canary?
a: Progressive delivery (Argo Rollouts/Flagger) automatically shifts traffic, queries metrics (error rate, latency), and promotes or rolls back on thresholds — no human in the loop. A plain canary needs a human to watch and promote.

q: Why decouple deploy from release with feature flags?
a: Code can be deployed (live but dark) without being released (flag off). Turning a flag on/off releases or instantly rolls back without a redeploy — separating the risky 'ship the bits' step from the 'expose to users' step.

q: What are the four DORA metrics and what do they measure?
a: Deployment frequency and lead time for changes measure THROUGHPUT; change failure rate and time to restore service measure STABILITY. Healthy teams improve both together.

q: What does concurrency with cancel-in-progress do in a workflow?
a: It groups runs (e.g. by ref) and cancels superseded in-flight runs so you don't waste minutes building stale commits when newer pushes arrive.
